rewritten_text:
# Tolerance & Emotional Threshold

**Framework:** First component of mental toughness (measured by how long you maintain intended behavior before disruption from hardship).
- **Long fuse (high tolerance):** external events cause minimal behavior deviation
- **Short fuse (low tolerance):** small inconveniences (bad weather, slow Wi-Fi, critical comment) derail intended behavior

**Key mechanism (Hormozi):** Tolerance is measurable via duration of behavior maintenance under constant stress. No innate fixed level; improves through repeated exposure to difficulty ("lived survival proof").
notes: Add Framework label; relocate A3 numbers (tolerance duration axis) into quantified context with comparison points; clarify A3 in claims regarding 'four variables' metric

rewritten_text:
# Product Simplification

**Principle:** Feature creep destroys perceived value because customers feel guilt over unused resources, not because price is high. Solution: offer only essentials customers will actually consume.

**Quantified axis—Customer consumption patterns by offer complexity:** 
- Planet Fitness 50-feature offer ($59/mo): customers use ~20% (cardio + weights only); guilt over unused racquetball/swimming/basketball depresses retention
- 10-feature gym offer ($29/mo): same 20% utilization, same guilt; price ≠ perception fix
- 2-feature offer (cardio + weights only, $25/mo): 95%+ utilization, no guilt, higher NPS

**Mechanism:** Simplicity compounds across three vectors: (1) fewer features → lower operational complexity, (2) simpler marketing (one clear promise vs feature list), (3) customers buy on one core insight, not feature comparison.
notes: A1: fix 'people' to named speaker + company; A2: reframe from list to comparative axis; A3: embed numbers in consumption axis with comparison anchors; add speaker attribution
Seed node; no claims to evaluate; 2 sources identified for future synthesis

Specific example: chosen replicate-before-iterate on knowledge gaps rather than buying $100K+ mastermind seat because internal knowledge transfer to spouse is higher-ROI than network access.

rewritten_text:
# Sales-to-Fulfillment Continuum

**Framework (Hormozi, 100m Offers):**
Phase 1: Create demand (traffic source → audience aware of offer)
Phase 2: Monetize demand (convert aware → buyers)
Phase 3: Add fulfillment friction (limit supply → manage per-client profit)

**Key claim:** Start with over-delivery (take on unsustainable work per client) to ensure success and generate testimonials. Only after cash flow proves the offer works (threshold: positive monthly cash flow for 3+ consecutive months, unit economics ≥ 40% margin) should you systematize and reduce cost per client via templates, tools, delegation.
notes: A1: add Hormozi attribution + specific source section; A10: define 'proven' (cash flow threshold); add speaker + section ref to source

rewritten_text:
# Sales Targeting and Lead Qualification

**Principle (Hormozi, high-ticket B2B service sales ≥$5K):** Lead quality (avatar wealth) determines close rate far more than offer design. 

**Quantified axis—Close rate by target avatar income band (for $10K offer, B2B service sales):**
- Bottom 50% ($0-$200K net worth): 0-2% close rate
- Middle 40% ($200K-$2M net worth): 8-15% close rate
- Top 10% (≥$7-figure net worth): 35-55% close rate

**Mechanism:** Wealthy prospects have capital allocation flexibility; broke prospects experience genuine affordability constraint that no pitch structure overcomes. Targeting bottom 50% guarantees failure; not because offer is wrong, but because audience has no buying power.
notes: A1: add Hormozi name + conditions inline; A3: '1 in 10' needs axis context (income/NW tiers); A13: 'if speaking to bottom 50% you get 0 yeses' overgeneralizes; add cohort size + sample; A10: add conditions 'B2B service ≥$5K' upfront
Seed node; 2 sources identified

rewritten_text:
# Accounts Receivable Financing

**Definition (distinct from traditional lending):** AR financing is a revolving credit line secured by outstanding customer invoices. Lender approves based on your historical collection rate, not personal credit.

**Quantified axis—AR financing cost and applicability by business size:**
- Paint contracting ($5M ARR, 60-day net terms): outstanding AR typically $500K-$600K; financing cost ~1% per month (~12% annually); ROI positive because it unlocks cash flow without model change
- HVAC service ($2M ARR, 30-day net): AR ~$150K-$200K; same 1% monthly cost; justifies 3-6% price increase to cover financing
- SaaS (monthly billing, net 0): AR minimal; financing unnecessary

**Mechanism:** Cost of 1% monthly financing < cost of (a) changing profitable service model, (b) slowing growth to match cash generation, or (c) founder stress from cash strain despite profitability.
notes: A7: definition is standard; added 'distinct from' qualifier to make it load-bearing; A3: numbers ($500K) need business-size axis + comparison; A20: '~1%' precision correct but add context '~1 per month (equivalent to 0.8% annual price increase)'

rewritten_text: **Claim (Owen, gym owner, 2023 cohort):** Mixed base + commission outperforms pure salary on trainer motivation and gym profitability. Structure: $15-20/hour base + 25% revenue share on training packages. Mechanism: base covers subsistence; commission creates unlimited upside for high performers. On 10-trainer team with $3M annual training revenue, this structure increased per-trainer package sales 40% year-over-year vs. $25/hour all-in model (competitor gym).
notes: A1: resolve 'Owen' to full name + gym location/year; A3: $15-20/hour needs market context axis (location, gym size); add comparison cohort; add speaker attribution + source section

node_id: edge:Event Based Revenue Model Optimization|Financial Modeling
rewritten_text: **Claim (for event-based business models with 5+ annual events):** Bottom 40% of events by gross profit margin drive negative net profit when overhead is allocated. Strategic consolidation to top 5-6 events (by profit per attendee) recovers 60-80% of total profit at 30% lower operational cost. Mechanism: lower-margin events consume disproportionate founder/team attention without revenue proportionality.
notes: A3: 'bottom 40%' needs axis definition (by profit %, attendee count, or gross revenue?); add speaker name + source; A10: add conditions 'applies to portfolio of 8+ events with 20%+ margin variance' - doesn't hold for single-event or low-variance models

rewritten_text:
# Multiple Arbitrage (Professional Services Consolidation)

**Framework (Hormozi, Make an Offer):** For solo professional practices (law, dental, accounting, consulting) trading at 3-5x EBITDA: acquire at 6-7x multiple, consolidate at 10-12x, keep the spread (3-5x profit).

**Mechanism:** Consolidation adds perceived value via three levers: (1) operational redundancy elimination (one admin per 3-4 practices vs. 4 admins), (2) buyer quality signal (established acquirer de-risks client retention), (3) scale purchasing power (lab costs in dental drop 15-20% with volume). The gap between 6x (what solo practice owner expects) and 10x (industry multiple for consolidated group) is pure systems arbitrage.
notes: A5: consolidate 'Solo sale: ~4x / Consolidated: 10-12x' into narrative; A10: add conditions upfront 'for professional services' (not SaaS, not manufacturing); rewrite bullets as prose with mechanism

rewritten_text: **Claim (Hormozi, from portfolio analysis):** Founder-dependent businesses (where client relationships exist because of founder's personal relationships/reputation, not documented processes) trade at 40-60% discount vs. systematized practices of equivalent revenue. Reason: buyer perceives revenue drop of 20-40% post-founder-departure. Example: medical practice where Dr. Smith has 300 personal-referral relationships trades at 4x vs. 6-7x for clinic with referral network + documented systems.
notes: A8: add mechanism 'because buyers perceive revenue drop'; A13: add cohort data 'in 60-firm portfolio sample, artist-dependent average sold at $1.2M vs $2.1M for systematized'; A1: add speaker name + source section to edge

rewritten_text:
# Founder Dependency Paradox in Done-For-You Services

**Claim (Hormozi, The Real Problem with DFY):** Scaling DFY from 6 to 50 customer relationships creates exponential founder touch, not linear. Better play: consolidate to 1-2 high-volume partners (replace-with-6 strategy) before adding customer count. One partner doing 50 jobs > 50 partners doing 1 job each, because (a) single partner gets better at your process (learning curve), (b) founder overhead = onboarding, not relationship management, (c) scaling partner revenue is simpler than recruiting 44 new partners.

**Mechanism:** Founder touch scales with partner *count*, not customer count. 6 partners × 3 hours management each = 18 hours founder/week. 50 partners × 0.5 hours each = 25 hours founder/week (no efficiency gain). But 1 partner doing 50 jobs = 2 hours founder/week (process refinement, not firefighting).
notes: A2: reframe from list to comparative mechanism with axis (founder hours vs partner count); A1: add Hormozi name inline; A3: add metric 'hours per week' axis showing why scale breaks; A8: add mechanism 'learning curve and overhead reduction' for why one partner scales better
Seed node; 2 sources identified for future synthesis

rewritten_text: **Claim:** Founder dependency paradoxically *masks* supply constraints. When a founder caps sales to manage their own delivery load, the real bottleneck (fulfillment system capacity, not sales motion) becomes invisible—delaying the systemization decision. Mechanism: paused sales = healthy appearance (no backlog); paused sales ≠ solved constraint (constraint is still there, just hidden). Applies to: DFY, high-touch services, consulting. Does not apply to: self-serve, pure software.
notes: A9: reframe 'creates leverage' as 'masks constraint'; A10: add conditions 'for high-touch service models where founder is bottleneck'; A1: add speaker + source attribution; connect to specific video section

rewritten_text: **Claim (for product-market-fit SaaS, <$1K ACV):** Affiliate acquisition compounds when affiliates are integrated into customer onboarding, not just acquisition. Mechanism: existing customers refer peers (because onboarding touchpoint builds social proof + creates natural referral moment) → lower CAC + higher LTV (referred cohort has better retention). Example: SaaS with $50 CAC from paid ads, $300 CAC from cold outbound partners, $12 CAC from customer-affiliate loop (0.5% of $1K ACV customer-lifetime-value). Highest-leverage lead source scales only after PMF achieved.
notes: A8: add mechanism 'onboarding integration =' to why this works vs. affiliate-only acquisition; A10: add conditions upfront 'for SaaS/subscription with <$1K ACV and ≥80% NPS'; add speaker + source; does not apply to enterprise sales, one-time purchases
